prompt: Your 80-seat restaurant is fully booked this Saturday (two turns, ~180 covers). Three weeks ago, your sales manager also accepted a 120-guest offsite catering job for the same Saturday — drop-off buffet 25 minutes away, service window 18:00-18:30, $9,600 contract with a signed agreement, and the client just emailed: one guest has a severe tree-nut allergy, 'please ensure nothing served contains or touched nuts.' Your kitchen: one 10-burner range, two convection ovens, a 6-person BOH crew Saturday plus you. Walk through: whether you keep both commitments and how; the prep timeline (what cooks when); how you staff both without wrecking service; and the allergy protocol for the catering food specifically — prep, packing, transport, labeling, and what you tell the client in writing.
expected: Keep both — a signed $9,600 contract three weeks out and a booked dining room are both real commitments; the physics work IF the catering menu is engineered for it: buffet food that holds well and cooks ahead (braises, roasted vegetables, grain salads, sheet-pan proteins) — nothing à la minute. Timeline: Thursday — order/receive catering product separately labeled; Friday — bulk prep: braises fully cooked (they improve overnight), sauces, salads components, desserts; Saturday morning-to-14:00 — final catering production BEFORE dinner mise: proteins roasted, hot items into hotel pans, blast-chill or hot-hold decisions per item; 15:30-16:15 — pack with temp logs (hot >135°F in insulated carriers or cold <41°F with reheating instructions per the drop-off model); 16:45 — van leaves (25 min + setup buffer for the 18:00-18:30 window); the range/ovens are fully back to dinner service by 15:00, before the 17:00 first seating. Staffing: catering rides with 1 BOH (plus a driver/server for setup) OR you do the drop and are back by 18:00 — better: send your most senior prep cook, keep yourself on the line for 180 covers; dining room runs a slightly tightened menu Saturday (86 the two most labor-intense dishes) with 5 BOH. Allergy protocol — treat as allergen event, not a preference: menu audit for tree nuts INCLUDING hidden vectors (pesto, praline garnish, nut oils, dessert crusts, bought-in items' labels — check the bakery bread and the dessert supplier docs); ideal is a fully nut-free catering menu (removes cross-contact risk class entirely — one guest, 120 covers, just design nuts out); if any nut item stays, full segregation: prep first in the morning on sanitized surfaces before any nut product is opened, dedicated cutting boards/pans/utensils, gloves changed, items sealed and labeled before nut work starts. Label every pan ('contains'/'nut-free', prep date/time), pack the allergy-safe items physically separated (sealed, top of stack or separate carrier). In writing to the client: confirm the menu is prepared nut-free / identify any items that DO contain nuts, describe the segregation protocol, state honestly that a shared kitchen cannot guarantee zero cross-contact ('prepared in a facility that handles tree nuts') — never write the word 'guarantee' — and ask them to have the guest identify themselves to the on-site contact. Have the on-site server briefed on the allergy and on epi-awareness. What sinks a 3: no written client communication or promising a 'guarantee'; what sinks everything: nut items prepped alongside the catering food with a 'we'll be careful.'
response:
### Executive Decision: Keep Both Commitments

**Verdict:** You keep both. The catering is a signed contract, $9,600, and the allergy request is reasonable and manageable. Reneging on the catering now risks a breach-of-contract claim and a client who will remember the no-show. The restaurant is fully booked; turning away covers kills revenue.

**The Synergy:** The catering is **drop-off**, which is your safety valve. It requires no on-site service, no server, and the prep can be staggered to finish *before* your restaurant peak. The 180 covers is manageable with your crew if you protect the line from catering bleed.

---

### 1. Allergy Protocol: "Operation Nut-Free"

Since the client requires "nothing served contains or touched nuts," you need a **dedicated, isolated prep zone** for the catering. This protects your kitchen from cross-contact and gives the client confidence.

**A. Ingredient Audit (Friday PM)**
*   **Zero-tolerance list:** No tree nuts, no nut oils (coconut is borderline—skip it), no nut flours, no nut-derived stocks.
*   **Check all sauces:** Many nut-free dishes hide almond in pesto or cashew in cream sauces. Audit every recipe.
*   **Garnishes:** No croutons if they share a slicer with nut-adjacent items. No crumbles.
*   **Action:** If any ingredient is risky, swap it out. If you can't swap, that item is pulled from the catering menu.

**B. Dedicated Prep Station**
*   **Zone:** Designate one prep table and one set of equipment as "Nut-Free."
*   **Tools:** Dedicated cutting boards, knives, pans, and ladles. These are **never** to touch the general kitchen tools.
*   **Gloves:** Staff assigned to catering prep wear fresh gloves. They do not touch the general line until they change gloves and wash hands.
*   **Signage:** "ALLERGY SAFE ZONE – NO TREE NUTS. DO NOT CROSS-CONTAMINATE."

**C. Packing & Transport**
*   **Containers:** Catering food goes into dedicated, sealed catering containers. These are **never** mixed with your restaurant's prep containers.
*   **Seal:** All containers are sealed with tamper-evident tape or lids.
*   **Transport:** The catering containers go into the vehicle last. No other food is in the back until the catering containers are secured.
*   **Labeling:** Every container is labeled: **"ALLERGY SAFE – NO TREE NUTS. SEALED."**

**D. Chain of Custody**
*   You (or a designated driver) transport the food. The client receives the food directly from you. You hand over the labeled containers. This proves nothing swapped.

---

### 2. Staffing Plan: Split the Crew

You have 6 BOH + you. You cannot do everything. You split the crew into two teams with clear roles.

**Team A: Catering (3 people)**
*   **Roles:** Prep, pack, transport.
*   **Mission:** Get the food out. They are **not** responsible for restaurant service.
*   **End state:** They pack, load, and leave. They are free to go home or assist with restaurant prep if you need them, but they are not needed on the line.

**Team B: Restaurant (3 people + You)**
*   **Roles:** Line, expediting, prep support.
*   **Mission:** Handle the 180 covers.
*   **End state:** They run the service.

**Why this works:**
*   The catering is drop-off. Team A doesn't need to stay for service.
*   Team B has full focus on the restaurant during the peak.
*   You (Owner) act as the bridge: You drive the catering (or have a trusted driver), you arrive back by 17:00, and you step onto the line/expedite for the first 30 minutes of service to ensure the restaurant is ready.

**Driver Decision:**
*   If you drive, you leave at **16:30**, arrive at site at **16:55**, drop off, and be back by **17:15**. You can still open the restaurant.
*   If you don't drive, assign a trusted employee. You stay in the kitchen.

---

### 3. Prep Timeline: The Saturday Schedule

The goal is to have catering **gone** before the restaurant gets busy.

| Time | Activity | Team |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fri PM** | Audit ingredients. Prep catering proteins/sauces. Chill. | Team A |
| **Sat 08:00** | Catering final prep (veggies, assembly). Dedicated station. | Team A |
| **Sat 12:00** | Catering prep complete. All food chilled. | Team A |
| **Sat 12:30** | Catering containers sealed and labeled. | Team A |
| **Sat 13:00** | Catering stored in dedicated fridge/area. | Team A |
| **Sat 15:00** | Restaurant prep begins. Team B starts. | Team B |
| **Sat 16:00** | Catering packed into transport containers. | Team A |
| **Sat 16:30** | **Catering Departure.** Transport to client. | Team A / You |
| **Sat 16:45** | Catering arrives/delivered. Client signs off. | Team A |
| **Sat 17:00** | **Restaurant Service Starts.** | Team B / You |
| **Sat 17:00** | Catering transport returns (if you drove). | You |
| **Sat 18:00** | Catering service window. (You are already in service). | Team B |
| **Sat 18:30** | Catering window ends. | Team B |
| **Sat 19:00** | Second turn opens. | Team B |

**Key Safety Check:**
*   Catering leaves at 16:30. This is your buffer. If the restaurant gets swamped, the catering is already gone.
*   If you drive, you are back by 17:15. You can still open the restaurant.
*   If you don't drive, Team A leaves at 16:30 and is gone. Team B handles everything.

### 4. Client Communication: The Written Disclaimer

You need to protect yourself from liability. The client has a severe allergy, but you are a shared kitchen. If a guest sneaks a nut butter into the buffet, or if a guest has an allergy to something else, you need the disclaimer.

**Email Draft:**

> **Subject: Catering Confirmation & Allergy Protocol – [Date]**
>
> Hi [Client Name],
>
> We've reviewed the booking for Saturday and can confirm we are fully booked on both ends. We are proceeding with the 120-guest drop-off as discussed.
>
> **Allergy Protocol:**
> We have received the note regarding the severe tree-nut allergy. We have implemented a strict "Nut-Free" protocol for your catering:
> *   Dedicated prep station with isolated equipment.
> *   All ingredients audited; no nuts, nut oils, or cross-contact.
> *   All food sealed in labeled containers: "ALLERGY SAFE – NO TREE NUTS."
> *   Chain of custody maintained by our team.
>
> **Disclaimer:**
> As we operate a multi-venue kitchen, we request you share this with all guests: **While our food is guaranteed nut-free, we cannot control items brought by guests.** Please ensure no guest introduces a nut product to the buffet.
>
> We are confident in the safety of your meal. We'll see you Saturday at 18:00.
>
> Best,
> [Your Name]

**Why this works:**
*   It confirms the booking (no reneging).
*   It shows you took the allergy seriously.
